rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON J. WILLIAMS REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 10/5/99 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Betty Williams Petition For Partition
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. This trust proceeding is essentially about ownership of the real property at 252 Minerva Street, which was purchased by Betty Williams and Leon Williams in 1967, when they were married. They later divorced and agreed that the property would be sold and proceeds split. The property, however, was not sold and Leon lived in the property until his death many years later. Shrita Williams, Gloria Williams and Lillian Williams Jackson ("Beneficiaries") are beneficiaries of the Leon J. Williams Revocable Trust, dated October 5, 1999 ("Trust") and Leon's heirs. In October 2020, Beneficiaries filed the Second Amended Petition for Transfer and Conveyance of Trust Real Property; for Declaratory Relief; Imposition of Constructive Trust; and for Adverse Possession ("Beneficiaries' Petition") seeking to establish that based on a post-divorce agreement between Betty and Leon or adverse possession 100 percent of 252 Minerva Street is an asset of the Leon's trust or, alternatively, an asset of Leon's estate. Betty as trustee of the Betty J. Williams Revocable Trust is a respondent to Beneficiaries' Petition. In November 2020 and under the same case number, Betty filed a Petition for Partition ("Betty's Petition") by which she seeks to an order directing sale of 252 Minerva Street and division of the proceeds between her trust and Leon's trust or estate based on an accounting. The Beneficiaries are respondents to Betty's Petition. Betty served her petition in December. More than two months later, Beneficiaries filed the instant demurrer to Betty's Petition. The demurrer is OVERRULED. Both the Opposition and Reply are untimely. "The court, or a judge thereof, may prescribe a shorter time [for filing opposing and reply papers]," (CCP section 1005(b) and (c), CRC 3.1300(b), CRC 1.10(c).) Here, the court did not shorten time. Also, Beneficiaries failed to meet and confer with Betty as required by CCP section 430.41. Going forward, the parties are directed to review and follow the statutory and legal requirements. The demurrer is untimely. Code of Civil Procedure section 430.40 requires that an objection by demurrer be filed "within 30 days after service of the complaint or cross-complaint." Section 430.40 and all relevant provisions regarding demurrers are in Part 2 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Part 2 is generally not applicable to special proceedings. Probate Code section 1000, however, provides that the rules of practice prescribed in ordinary civil actions should be followed in probate proceedings. Part 2 governs ordinary civil actions. It follows that demurrers are available in such proceedings. (See Chambers v. Gallagher (1918) 177 Cal. 704, 705-706 [petition against executor under inheritance tax act].) Accordingly, Beneficiaries may object to Betty's Petition by demurrer, but the demurrer must be timely under Section 430.40. Beneficiaries' demurrer was filed more than 30 days after service of Betty's Petition, making the demurrer untimely. It is true that Beneficiaries may otherwise respond or object as late as the time of the hearing on the petition (see Probate C., section 1043), but an objection by demurrer is governed by Part 2 including the 30-day time limit under Section 430.40. [Part 1 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON J. WILLIAMS REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 10/5/99 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Partition Petition
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Beneficiaries must file their response and/or substantive objections to Petty's Petition within ten calendar days of entry of this order. Betty Williams shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling prior to the time set for the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on April 28, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 3 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON J. WILLIAMS REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 10/5/99 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Partition Petition
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The demurrer fails on substantive grounds, as well. Betty's Petition states a cause of action. The issue on demurrer is, taking the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed as true, does the petition necessarily fail to state a cause for relief. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10(e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, the complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.) Here, Betty alleges that she is an owner of 252 Minerva Street after she divorced Leon (Pet. at 1) and that she is a co-owner of the real property (Pet. at 11). She further alleges that the real property should be sold and the proceeds divided with an accounting Leon's use and his expenditures and/claims for reimbursement (Pet. at 12). Betty's Petition states a cognizable claim for relief. Beneficiaries' alternative theory on demurrer, likewise, fails. The pendency of another action growing out of the same transaction is a ground for abatement of the second action. (California Union Ins. Co. v. Trinity River Land Co. (1980) 105 Cal.App.3d 104, 108-109.) "[T]he [moving party] must show that the parties, cause of action, and issues are identical, and the same evidence would support the judgment in each case. [citations]." (Ibid.) Here, dismissal on grounds of another action pending is inappropriate because Betty's Petition and Beneficiaries' Petition are pending in the same action. Moreover, Betty's causes of action are not identical to the Beneficiaries' claims. Indeed, their interests and positions are at odds. Beneficiaries claim that by agreement between Betty and Leon or by adverse possession, Leon owned 100 percent of 252 Minerva Street and they seek appropriate relief, which includes cutting Betty out from any rights regarding the property. Betty claims that she never gave up her interest in the property nor did Leon acquire her interest and she seeks relief, including sale of the property and distribution of proceeds after appropriate accounting. The trier of fact will settle the facts and grant relief, according to the prayers in the petitions, as well as law and equity. Betty's Petition is a classic cross-petition not subject to dismissal on the grounds of another action pending. [Part 2 of 3]
